title: Inferred pointer declarations retain the caret on the binding
summary: auto* bindings render as p^ := value while explicit T* bindings render as p: T^ = value.
details: auto* p = value becomes mut p^ := value for a mutable local.
const auto* p = value becomes p^ := value.
T* p = value becomes mut p: T^ = value.
The caret on an inferred binding records the source-level auto* deduction constraint; it is not pretending to be the complete deduced type.
Equals remains the explicit-type initializer marker and colon-equals remains the inferred-type initializer marker.
rationale: Dropping the caret would hide that auto* rejects non-pointer deduction even though plain auto accepts it.
Type-side partial spellings such as p: ^ := value and split spellings such as mut p^: T = value were rejected as visually unacceptable.
alternatives: mut p := value hides the auto* constraint.
p: ^ := value preserves information but was rejected by the owner as visually unacceptable.
mut p^: T = value splits one type across both sides of the colon.

provenance.note: Owner explicitly selected the existing p^ := spelling after reviewing alternatives.

evidence.reference: tests/frontend_spec.lua::auto* ptr and const auto* ptr
evidence.claim: Focused render assertions pin inferred pointer spellings.

questions: Why is the caret attached to an auto pointer variable?
Should auto* and T* declarations render identically?
Why not hide the pointer marker when the initializer is obvious?

last_verified.method: Owner decision matched against focused frontend assertions.
